metadata.title: Catch a Package Hallucination Before It Becomes a Slopsquatting Attack

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: I like that the scanner checks each import against the PyPI JSON API and flags requests_oauth_helper as a 404 before pip runs. I want to harden it beyond a single-file check. Help me: (1) extend it to walk an entire project tree and dedupe imports so I audit every module at once; (2) handle edge cases like aliased imports, conditional imports, and packages whose install name differs from their import name; (3) add a fail-the-build exit code so the scanner blocks CI when any HALLUCINATED verdict appears.

props.command: I like that the scanner checks each import against the PyPI JSON API and flags requests_oauth_helper as a 404 before pip runs. I want to harden it beyond a single-file check. Help me: (1) extend it to walk an entire project tree and dedupe imports so I audit every module at once; (2) handle edge cases like aliased imports, conditional imports, and packages whose install name differs from their import name; (3) add a fail-the-build exit code so the scanner blocks CI when any HALLUCINATED verdict appears.
props.segment: Catch a Package Hallucination Before It

beats.narration_text: Claude imports requests_oauth_helper — a name that doesn't exist on PyPI today but might be squatted tomorrow with malicious code inside.

Hallucinated package names appear across thousands of model outputs. Slopsquatting works by registering those names with malicious payloads. The scanner catches the risk at the import line before pip install runs.
We ask Claude to write a package auditor that reads import statements from a file and checks each against the PyPI JSON API.

props.command:
claude "Write package_auditor.py:
  read Python file -> extract imports
  query PyPI JSON API per package
  report EXISTS / HALLUCINATED
  flag 404 as slopsquatting risk"

beats.narration_text: Claude writes the auditor using requests and the PyPI API. It extracts top-level package names only, handles both 'import X' and 'from X import Y', and prints a color-coded verdict per package.
Scanner run: requests — EXISTS. numpy — EXISTS. requests_oauth_helper — HALLUCINATED — 404. Slopsquatting risk flagged. The malicious payload would install on pip install without any warning.
We extend the scanner with a --npm flag for JavaScript imports using the npm registry API.

props.command:
claude "Add --npm flag:
  check npm registry API
  registry.npmjs.org/-/v1/search?text=<pkg>
  same EXISTS/HALLUCINATED output"

beats.narration_text: npm scan: react — EXISTS. lodash — EXISTS. react-query-optimizer-v3 — HALLUCINATED. The pattern is identical across ecosystems — any package name a model invents is a potential squatting target.
The hallucination rate is low but the recurrence rate is high — the same fake names appear across thousands of outputs. Scan imports before pip install, not after.
Next: write a five-artifact Software Design Document with Claude Code.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Here's what the body just demonstrated. beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Catch a Package Hallucination Before It Becomes a Slopsquatting Attack] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
